channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: MTD Deadline 7 August 2026: What UK Landlords Must File Now
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK landlords with qualifying property income should mark 7th August 2026 in their diaries, as this date represents the first major quarterly submission deadline under HMRC's Making Tax Digital (MTD) initiative for the 2026/27 tax year. The deadline covers the period from 6th April to 5th July 2026, requiring digital record-keeping and submission through compatible software.

Understanding the MTD Requirements

For landlords falling under MTD rules, this isn't simply a reminder but the first mandatory quarterly submission under the new digital tax system. The requirements apply to those with annual property income above £50,000 (for those already in the scheme) or £30,000 (for those being brought into scope). Key aspects include:

  • Maintaining digital records of all property income and expenses
  • Using MTD-compatible software to submit quarterly updates
  • Providing a final declaration after the tax year ends
  • Keeping records for at least five years after the relevant tax year

Preparing for the Transition

Landlords approaching the threshold or already within scope may wish to consider several preparatory steps:

  • Evaluating current record-keeping systems and transitioning to digital methods
  • Researching and selecting HMRC-approved MTD software solutions
  • Familiarising themselves with the quarterly submission process
  • Considering professional accounting support if managing complex portfolios

Whilst the 2026 deadline may seem distant, implementing these changes gradually could help avoid last-minute complications. Many accounting software providers now offer specific MTD solutions tailored for property investors.

Landlords with questions about their MTD obligations might find it helpful to consult HMRC guidance or seek advice from a qualified tax professional, particularly if managing multiple properties or complex financial arrangements. The shift to digital tax reporting represents a significant change in how landlords manage their tax affairs, making early preparation potentially valuable.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK landlords with qualifying property income should mark 7th August 2026 in their diaries, as this date represents the first major quarterly submission deadline under HMRC's Making Tax Digital (MTD) initiative for the 2026/27 tax year. The deadline covers the period from 6th April to 5th July 2026, requiring digital record-keeping and submission through compatible software.

Understanding the MTD Requirements

For landlords falling under MTD rules, this isn't simply a reminder but the first mandatory quarterly submission under the new digital tax system. The requirements apply to those with annual property income above £50,000 (for those already in the scheme) or £30,000 (for those being brought into scope). Key aspects include:

  • Maintaining digital records of all property income and expenses
  • Using MTD-compatible software to submit quarterly updates
  • Providing a final declaration after the tax year ends
  • Keeping records for at least five years after the relevant tax year

Preparing for the Transition

Landlords approaching the threshold or already within scope may wish to consider several preparatory steps:

  • Evaluating current record-keeping systems and transitioning to digital methods
  • Researching and selecting HMRC-approved MTD software solutions
  • Familiarising themselves with the quarterly submission process
  • Considering professional accounting support if managing complex portfolios

Whilst the 2026 deadline may seem distant, implementing these changes gradually could help avoid last-minute complications. Many accounting software providers now offer specific MTD solutions tailored for property investors.

Landlords with questions about their MTD obligations might find it helpful to consult HMRC guidance or seek advice from a qualified tax professional, particularly if managing multiple properties or complex financial arrangements. The shift to digital tax reporting represents a significant change in how landlords manage their tax affairs, making early preparation potentially valuable.

item.title: Lettings platform launches post-Renters’ Rights Act insurance products
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

As the rental market adapts to the Renters’ Rights Act, lettings platform Goodlord has partnered with Elevate Specialty to introduce new insurance products tailored to the evolving sector. The offerings include landlord protection and tenant income cover, designed to address the challenges of longer tenancies and updated possession rules.

New protections for landlords and tenants

The collaboration has produced two key products embedded within Goodlord’s platform. For landlords, a rent guarantee insurance provides cover against rental income loss and legal expenses – particularly valuable as tenancy agreements become more complex under recent reforms. Meanwhile, Goodlord Rent & Bills offers tenants income protection to help maintain rent and utility payments during periods when they’re unable to work.

These solutions have been developed specifically with the Renters’ Rights Act and Consumer Duty regulations in mind. Mickey Attia of Elevate Specialty noted: “The rental market is changing fast, and insurance needs to keep up. We’ve focused on early resolution and practical outcomes rather than bureaucratic delays.”

Simplifying protection in a changing market

By integrating these services directly into their platform, Goodlord aims to make essential protections more accessible. The company’s insurance director Oli Sherlock explained: “Landlords and agents want solutions that reflect how the market operates today. Embedding these services creates simpler, more relevant protection throughout the tenancy lifecycle.”

The move comes as industry participants adjust to significant regulatory changes. Landlords may find the new insurance products particularly useful given the act’s modifications to possession processes and tenancy durations. Similarly, tenants facing greater job market uncertainty might consider income protection as a way to safeguard their housing stability.

As the rental sector continues to evolve, such innovations could play an important role in balancing the needs of landlords and tenants while complying with new legal requirements.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

As the rental market adapts to the Renters’ Rights Act, lettings platform Goodlord has partnered with Elevate Specialty to introduce new insurance products tailored to the evolving sector. The offerings include landlord protection and tenant income cover, designed to address the challenges of longer tenancies and updated possession rules.

New protections for landlords and tenants

The collaboration has produced two key products embedded within Goodlord’s platform. For landlords, a rent guarantee insurance provides cover against rental income loss and legal expenses – particularly valuable as tenancy agreements become more complex under recent reforms. Meanwhile, Goodlord Rent & Bills offers tenants income protection to help maintain rent and utility payments during periods when they’re unable to work.

These solutions have been developed specifically with the Renters’ Rights Act and Consumer Duty regulations in mind. Mickey Attia of Elevate Specialty noted: “The rental market is changing fast, and insurance needs to keep up. We’ve focused on early resolution and practical outcomes rather than bureaucratic delays.”

Simplifying protection in a changing market

By integrating these services directly into their platform, Goodlord aims to make essential protections more accessible. The company’s insurance director Oli Sherlock explained: “Landlords and agents want solutions that reflect how the market operates today. Embedding these services creates simpler, more relevant protection throughout the tenancy lifecycle.”

The move comes as industry participants adjust to significant regulatory changes. Landlords may find the new insurance products particularly useful given the act’s modifications to possession processes and tenancy durations. Similarly, tenants facing greater job market uncertainty might consider income protection as a way to safeguard their housing stability.

As the rental sector continues to evolve, such innovations could play an important role in balancing the needs of landlords and tenants while complying with new legal requirements.

item.title: Conveyancing platform donates record amount to charity partners
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Conveyancing platform Conveybuddy has reported its highest-ever quarterly charitable donation, with £9,747 distributed to three UK charities between April and June this year. The record contribution comes as more mortgage advisers use the platform to process conveyancing cases, with each completed transaction generating up to £5 for good causes.

How the charitable initiative works

The platform operates a unique giving model where:

  • For every completed conveyancing case, Conveybuddy donates up to £5 to charity
  • Advisers using the platform can select which of the three partner charities receives their contribution
  • Donations are distributed quarterly to the Motor Neurone Disease Association, Rainbows Hospice and Food Bank Aid

This quarter's record donation follows increased adviser registrations and transaction volumes on the platform. Since launching the initiative, Conveybuddy has maintained its commitment to supporting charities that make a tangible difference across the UK.

A growing impact through conveyancing

Harpal Singh, Conveybuddy's chief executive, commented: "From day one, we wanted our platform to have a meaningful charitable dimension – one where advisers could directly influence which causes benefit from their work. Seeing these donations grow alongside our user base shows how the property sector can create positive change."

The platform's model demonstrates how mortgage and conveyancing services can incorporate charitable giving without affecting costs for homebuyers. Advisers using the system continue their normal workflow while automatically generating donations through completed transactions.

For homeowners going through the property purchase process, working with advisers who use charitable platforms like Conveybuddy could be a way to indirectly support good causes. However, borrowers might still wish to compare conveyancing services based on factors like fees, speed and customer service when making their selection.

As the platform grows, its charitable contributions may continue to increase – potentially benefiting more UK charities in future quarters. The latest donation highlights how innovative models in the property sector can create financial support for vital causes while maintaining efficient service for clients.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Conveyancing platform Conveybuddy has reported its highest-ever quarterly charitable donation, with £9,747 distributed to three UK charities between April and June this year. The record contribution comes as more mortgage advisers use the platform to process conveyancing cases, with each completed transaction generating up to £5 for good causes.

How the charitable initiative works

The platform operates a unique giving model where:

  • For every completed conveyancing case, Conveybuddy donates up to £5 to charity
  • Advisers using the platform can select which of the three partner charities receives their contribution
  • Donations are distributed quarterly to the Motor Neurone Disease Association, Rainbows Hospice and Food Bank Aid

This quarter's record donation follows increased adviser registrations and transaction volumes on the platform. Since launching the initiative, Conveybuddy has maintained its commitment to supporting charities that make a tangible difference across the UK.

A growing impact through conveyancing

Harpal Singh, Conveybuddy's chief executive, commented: "From day one, we wanted our platform to have a meaningful charitable dimension – one where advisers could directly influence which causes benefit from their work. Seeing these donations grow alongside our user base shows how the property sector can create positive change."

The platform's model demonstrates how mortgage and conveyancing services can incorporate charitable giving without affecting costs for homebuyers. Advisers using the system continue their normal workflow while automatically generating donations through completed transactions.

For homeowners going through the property purchase process, working with advisers who use charitable platforms like Conveybuddy could be a way to indirectly support good causes. However, borrowers might still wish to compare conveyancing services based on factors like fees, speed and customer service when making their selection.

As the platform grows, its charitable contributions may continue to increase – potentially benefiting more UK charities in future quarters. The latest donation highlights how innovative models in the property sector can create financial support for vital causes while maintaining efficient service for clients.

item.title: Rayner urged to rule out rent controls and help buyers stuck in limbo
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Angela Rayner’s reappointment as housing secretary has drawn mixed reactions from property industry leaders, with calls to prioritise housing supply while avoiding rent controls. The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA) welcomed her return but stressed the need for balanced reforms in the private rented sector that support both tenants and responsible landlords.

Industry calls for practical rental reforms

The NRLA has urged the government to focus on increasing housing stock while ensuring fair treatment for landlords and tenants. Chief executive Ben Beadle highlighted the importance of strengthening enforcement against rogue landlords and improving court processes to support the Renters’ Rights Act. He cautioned against rent controls, citing concerns they could reduce rental property availability when demand remains high.

Leasehold reform specialists have also responded positively to Rayner’s reappointment. The Association of Leasehold Enfranchisement Practitioners (ALEP) noted her previous experience with leasehold legislation could help maintain momentum on reforms. With Matthew Pennycook remaining as housing minister, industry experts anticipate continuity in housing policy direction.

Addressing the housing supply challenge

Property professionals agree that boosting housing supply should be a top priority for the new government. The NRLA emphasised the need for policies that encourage investment in rental properties while protecting tenant rights. Industry bodies suggest focusing on practical measures like speeding up planning processes and supporting responsible landlords, rather than implementing rent controls which could deter investment.

For homeowners and prospective buyers, these developments may signal ongoing changes in housing policy. Those affected by leasehold reforms or renting issues might monitor how the government balances tenant protections with maintaining a healthy rental market. With experienced ministers returning to their housing roles, borrowers could see more consistent policy approaches emerging in coming months.

As the new government settles in, housing market participants will be watching for concrete proposals to address the UK’s property challenges. The balance between tenant protections and landlord rights, along with leasehold reforms, are likely to remain key areas of focus in the housing agenda.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Angela Rayner’s reappointment as housing secretary has drawn mixed reactions from property industry leaders, with calls to prioritise housing supply while avoiding rent controls. The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA) welcomed her return but stressed the need for balanced reforms in the private rented sector that support both tenants and responsible landlords.

Industry calls for practical rental reforms

The NRLA has urged the government to focus on increasing housing stock while ensuring fair treatment for landlords and tenants. Chief executive Ben Beadle highlighted the importance of strengthening enforcement against rogue landlords and improving court processes to support the Renters’ Rights Act. He cautioned against rent controls, citing concerns they could reduce rental property availability when demand remains high.

Leasehold reform specialists have also responded positively to Rayner’s reappointment. The Association of Leasehold Enfranchisement Practitioners (ALEP) noted her previous experience with leasehold legislation could help maintain momentum on reforms. With Matthew Pennycook remaining as housing minister, industry experts anticipate continuity in housing policy direction.

Addressing the housing supply challenge

Property professionals agree that boosting housing supply should be a top priority for the new government. The NRLA emphasised the need for policies that encourage investment in rental properties while protecting tenant rights. Industry bodies suggest focusing on practical measures like speeding up planning processes and supporting responsible landlords, rather than implementing rent controls which could deter investment.

For homeowners and prospective buyers, these developments may signal ongoing changes in housing policy. Those affected by leasehold reforms or renting issues might monitor how the government balances tenant protections with maintaining a healthy rental market. With experienced ministers returning to their housing roles, borrowers could see more consistent policy approaches emerging in coming months.

As the new government settles in, housing market participants will be watching for concrete proposals to address the UK’s property challenges. The balance between tenant protections and landlord rights, along with leasehold reforms, are likely to remain key areas of focus in the housing agenda.

item.title: Three little words – how to make a lawyer weep
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Conveyancing delays often frustrate homebuyers and sellers, with many quick to point fingers at solicitors. However, the reality behind transaction hold-ups is more complex than some might assume. Whilst legal professionals make convenient scapegoats, the truth reveals systemic challenges that affect all parties in the property chain.

The real bottlenecks in property transactions

Contrary to popular belief, missing paperwork like FENSA certificates or local searches rarely causes significant delays in modern conveyancing. The government's proposed Home Information Packs aim to address information gaps, but industry professionals report different pain points. The most time-consuming aspects often involve client-side processes: collecting identification documents, ensuring accurate form completion, and arranging initial payments.

Even with digital systems streamlining these tasks, onboarding clients typically takes around seven days on average. This slow start sets the tone for what can become a 14-week conveyancing marathon. During this extended period, buyers frequently reconsider their choices - whether switching properties, changing lenders, or simply getting cold feet. These client-side uncertainties create ongoing challenges for conveyancers trying to maintain transaction momentum.

The three words that halt progress

Among all potential delays, one phrase stands out as particularly problematic: "source of wealth". This anti-money laundering requirement has become a major sticking point in many transactions. Solicitors must thoroughly verify where buyers' deposit funds originate, which can involve extensive documentation requests and verification processes.

Whilst these checks are legally necessary, they often catch buyers unprepared. Common scenarios that trigger additional scrutiny include:

  • Gifted deposits from family members
  • Funds transferred from overseas accounts
  • Savings accumulated over many years without clear paper trails
  • Business earnings or investment profits

Proactive buyers might consider preparing these documents early when house hunting to prevent later delays. Having bank statements, gift letters, and evidence of fund origins ready can significantly smooth the conveyancing process.

Understanding these common hurdles helps all parties manage expectations during property transactions. Rather than assigning blame, buyers, sellers and professionals working collaboratively can help reduce unnecessary delays and stress in what remains a complex legal process.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Conveyancing delays often frustrate homebuyers and sellers, with many quick to point fingers at solicitors. However, the reality behind transaction hold-ups is more complex than some might assume. Whilst legal professionals make convenient scapegoats, the truth reveals systemic challenges that affect all parties in the property chain.

The real bottlenecks in property transactions

Contrary to popular belief, missing paperwork like FENSA certificates or local searches rarely causes significant delays in modern conveyancing. The government's proposed Home Information Packs aim to address information gaps, but industry professionals report different pain points. The most time-consuming aspects often involve client-side processes: collecting identification documents, ensuring accurate form completion, and arranging initial payments.

Even with digital systems streamlining these tasks, onboarding clients typically takes around seven days on average. This slow start sets the tone for what can become a 14-week conveyancing marathon. During this extended period, buyers frequently reconsider their choices - whether switching properties, changing lenders, or simply getting cold feet. These client-side uncertainties create ongoing challenges for conveyancers trying to maintain transaction momentum.

The three words that halt progress

Among all potential delays, one phrase stands out as particularly problematic: "source of wealth". This anti-money laundering requirement has become a major sticking point in many transactions. Solicitors must thoroughly verify where buyers' deposit funds originate, which can involve extensive documentation requests and verification processes.

Whilst these checks are legally necessary, they often catch buyers unprepared. Common scenarios that trigger additional scrutiny include:

  • Gifted deposits from family members
  • Funds transferred from overseas accounts
  • Savings accumulated over many years without clear paper trails
  • Business earnings or investment profits

Proactive buyers might consider preparing these documents early when house hunting to prevent later delays. Having bank statements, gift letters, and evidence of fund origins ready can significantly smooth the conveyancing process.

Understanding these common hurdles helps all parties manage expectations during property transactions. Rather than assigning blame, buyers, sellers and professionals working collaboratively can help reduce unnecessary delays and stress in what remains a complex legal process.

item.title: Buying beats renting on monthly costs in 41% of local markets, study finds
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals that monthly mortgage payments now undercut rental costs in 41% of local authority areas across England and Wales, with northern regions offering particularly strong value for first-time buyers. The Pepper Money First-Time Buyer Index, analysing ONS data, highlights where purchasing property could prove more cost-effective than renting when comparing housing costs against local earnings.

Northern regions dominate affordability rankings

The study found the North East and North West contain 13 of the top 20 most affordable locations for first-time buyers. Middlesbrough leads the rankings, followed by Burnley and Merthyr Tydfil, benefiting from relatively low house prices combined with favourable earnings-to-property-cost ratios. Every North East local authority except Northumberland showed lower estimated mortgage payments than equivalent rental costs.

Even in pricier northern cities like Manchester and Salford, where average property values exceed many regional neighbours, high rental prices mean buyers could potentially save over £3,000 annually by purchasing rather than renting. Southampton emerged as offering the most substantial annual savings among top-ranked areas, with potential buyers saving £3,924 compared to renting.

Narrowing gap between renting and buying costs

Across England and Wales, the average first-time buyer property costs £261,700, while the typical monthly rent for a three-bedroom home stands at £1,269. The research identified 94 additional local authorities where monthly ownership costs fall within £100 of rental payments, suggesting the financial case for buying becomes increasingly marginal in these areas.

Rugby presents an interesting exception in the top ten rankings, being the only location where renting remains cheaper than buying by £44 per month. Its inclusion reflects higher local wages relative to property prices rather than particularly low rental costs.

For prospective buyers weighing their options, these findings suggest regional variations play a crucial role in determining whether purchasing represents better value than renting. With mortgage costs and rental prices moving at different rates across the country, regular local market comparisons could help inform housing decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals that monthly mortgage payments now undercut rental costs in 41% of local authority areas across England and Wales, with northern regions offering particularly strong value for first-time buyers. The Pepper Money First-Time Buyer Index, analysing ONS data, highlights where purchasing property could prove more cost-effective than renting when comparing housing costs against local earnings.

Northern regions dominate affordability rankings

The study found the North East and North West contain 13 of the top 20 most affordable locations for first-time buyers. Middlesbrough leads the rankings, followed by Burnley and Merthyr Tydfil, benefiting from relatively low house prices combined with favourable earnings-to-property-cost ratios. Every North East local authority except Northumberland showed lower estimated mortgage payments than equivalent rental costs.

Even in pricier northern cities like Manchester and Salford, where average property values exceed many regional neighbours, high rental prices mean buyers could potentially save over £3,000 annually by purchasing rather than renting. Southampton emerged as offering the most substantial annual savings among top-ranked areas, with potential buyers saving £3,924 compared to renting.

Narrowing gap between renting and buying costs

Across England and Wales, the average first-time buyer property costs £261,700, while the typical monthly rent for a three-bedroom home stands at £1,269. The research identified 94 additional local authorities where monthly ownership costs fall within £100 of rental payments, suggesting the financial case for buying becomes increasingly marginal in these areas.

Rugby presents an interesting exception in the top ten rankings, being the only location where renting remains cheaper than buying by £44 per month. Its inclusion reflects higher local wages relative to property prices rather than particularly low rental costs.

For prospective buyers weighing their options, these findings suggest regional variations play a crucial role in determining whether purchasing represents better value than renting. With mortgage costs and rental prices moving at different rates across the country, regular local market comparisons could help inform housing decisions.

item.title: Property auction raises £41m as buyer demand holds firm
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Property auctions continue to show resilience amidst broader economic uncertainty, with Savills' latest event raising over £41 million across residential and commercial lots. The two-day auction saw strong competition for redevelopment opportunities and income-generating assets, achieving a 68% success rate with 139 properties sold.

Strong demand for value-add opportunities

The auction highlighted particular interest in properties offering clear potential for value enhancement. A block of 12 one-bedroom flats in Hampton Wick sold for £3.235 million – £435,000 above its guide price – while a Hammersmith redevelopment site fetched £935,000. These results suggest buyers remain willing to pay premiums for assets with clear refurbishment or development potential.

Commercial properties accounted for 28% of total sales, including notable transactions such as a Worthing shopping centre and office investment selling for £1.62 million. The diversity of successful lots indicates sustained appetite across different property types, with investors particularly drawn to assets offering stable income streams.

Market resilience despite uncertainty

Savills reported robust bidding activity despite recent political developments, suggesting auction markets may be less susceptible to short-term uncertainty than traditional sales channels. The sale included several off-market transactions, including an eight-bedroom Essex property which sold just below its £1.3 million guide price prior to auction.

Gary Murphy, Savills Auctions director, noted: "The strongest interest focused on assets offering clear opportunities to add value. We also saw significant demand for income-producing commercial investments, highlighting the appeal of secure returns in the current climate." The results reflect a market where buyers remain active for well-priced opportunities, particularly those with potential for capital growth or reliable yields.

With commercial assets performing strongly alongside residential lots, the auction demonstrates how different buyer motivations – from development potential to income generation – continue to drive activity even during periods of wider market caution.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Property auctions continue to show resilience amidst broader economic uncertainty, with Savills' latest event raising over £41 million across residential and commercial lots. The two-day auction saw strong competition for redevelopment opportunities and income-generating assets, achieving a 68% success rate with 139 properties sold.

Strong demand for value-add opportunities

The auction highlighted particular interest in properties offering clear potential for value enhancement. A block of 12 one-bedroom flats in Hampton Wick sold for £3.235 million – £435,000 above its guide price – while a Hammersmith redevelopment site fetched £935,000. These results suggest buyers remain willing to pay premiums for assets with clear refurbishment or development potential.

Commercial properties accounted for 28% of total sales, including notable transactions such as a Worthing shopping centre and office investment selling for £1.62 million. The diversity of successful lots indicates sustained appetite across different property types, with investors particularly drawn to assets offering stable income streams.

Market resilience despite uncertainty

Savills reported robust bidding activity despite recent political developments, suggesting auction markets may be less susceptible to short-term uncertainty than traditional sales channels. The sale included several off-market transactions, including an eight-bedroom Essex property which sold just below its £1.3 million guide price prior to auction.

Gary Murphy, Savills Auctions director, noted: "The strongest interest focused on assets offering clear opportunities to add value. We also saw significant demand for income-producing commercial investments, highlighting the appeal of secure returns in the current climate." The results reflect a market where buyers remain active for well-priced opportunities, particularly those with potential for capital growth or reliable yields.

With commercial assets performing strongly alongside residential lots, the auction demonstrates how different buyer motivations – from development potential to income generation – continue to drive activity even during periods of wider market caution.

item.title: Viability pressures threaten regional build-to-rent pipeline
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Regional build-to-rent (BTR) developments are facing growing financial pressures, according to Knight Frank’s latest market analysis, with investment concentrating in major cities whilst smaller locations struggle with viability challenges. The research highlights a widening gap between London, Manchester and Birmingham – which accounted for nearly half of this year’s 6,700 completed BTR homes – and tier 2 cities like Liverpool and Sheffield, where just 14% of new stock was delivered.

Development challenges in regional markets

Knight Frank’s report suggests many regional BTR schemes require additional support to become financially viable, including government grants, relaxed Section 106 obligations or adjusted affordable housing requirements. Lizzie Breckner, Knight Frank’s head of residential investment research, notes that whilst overall supply is increasing, rising construction costs and tighter profit margins are making it particularly difficult to launch projects outside major urban centres.

The data shows 50,000 BTR homes currently under construction nationwide, with a further 125,000 in planning. However, delivery remains concentrated in areas with the strongest rental demand, leaving some regional markets underserved. Industry experts suggest this imbalance could persist unless economic conditions for developers improve in less central locations.

Record investment meets sector challenges

Despite these development hurdles, the BTR sector attracted £2.08bn of investment in Q2 2024 – a record quarterly figure. The UK’s completed BTR stock now stands at 166,359 homes, representing 17% annual growth. Nick Pleydell-Bouverie, Knight Frank’s head of residential investment, emphasises the sector’s strong fundamentals, with high occupancy rates and rental growth driven by ongoing demand for quality rental properties.

However, Pleydell-Bouverie cautions that maintaining this growth requires addressing financial viability concerns. Investors remain keen to deploy capital into BTR, but carefully assess whether individual schemes can deliver acceptable returns – a calculation that currently favours developments in major cities with proven rental demand.

For prospective renters, these market dynamics may mean fewer new BTR options in regional locations compared to larger cities. Whilst the sector continues to expand overall, its geographical distribution appears increasingly shaped by development economics as much as housing need.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Regional build-to-rent (BTR) developments are facing growing financial pressures, according to Knight Frank’s latest market analysis, with investment concentrating in major cities whilst smaller locations struggle with viability challenges. The research highlights a widening gap between London, Manchester and Birmingham – which accounted for nearly half of this year’s 6,700 completed BTR homes – and tier 2 cities like Liverpool and Sheffield, where just 14% of new stock was delivered.

Development challenges in regional markets

Knight Frank’s report suggests many regional BTR schemes require additional support to become financially viable, including government grants, relaxed Section 106 obligations or adjusted affordable housing requirements. Lizzie Breckner, Knight Frank’s head of residential investment research, notes that whilst overall supply is increasing, rising construction costs and tighter profit margins are making it particularly difficult to launch projects outside major urban centres.

The data shows 50,000 BTR homes currently under construction nationwide, with a further 125,000 in planning. However, delivery remains concentrated in areas with the strongest rental demand, leaving some regional markets underserved. Industry experts suggest this imbalance could persist unless economic conditions for developers improve in less central locations.

Record investment meets sector challenges

Despite these development hurdles, the BTR sector attracted £2.08bn of investment in Q2 2024 – a record quarterly figure. The UK’s completed BTR stock now stands at 166,359 homes, representing 17% annual growth. Nick Pleydell-Bouverie, Knight Frank’s head of residential investment, emphasises the sector’s strong fundamentals, with high occupancy rates and rental growth driven by ongoing demand for quality rental properties.

However, Pleydell-Bouverie cautions that maintaining this growth requires addressing financial viability concerns. Investors remain keen to deploy capital into BTR, but carefully assess whether individual schemes can deliver acceptable returns – a calculation that currently favours developments in major cities with proven rental demand.

For prospective renters, these market dynamics may mean fewer new BTR options in regional locations compared to larger cities. Whilst the sector continues to expand overall, its geographical distribution appears increasingly shaped by development economics as much as housing need.

item.title: Rayner reinstalled as housing secretary by new PM Burnham
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Angela Rayner has been reappointed as housing secretary in Prime Minister Andy Burnham’s new cabinet. This marks her return to the role after stepping down in September 2025 due to a stamp duty issue linked to her property purchase in Hove. Following an investigation by HMRC earlier this year, Rayner was cleared of any deliberate wrongdoing or negligence and has since repaid approximately £40,000 in underpaid stamp duty. She now replaces Steve Reed as secretary of state for housing, communities and local government.

This reshuffle comes as part of Burnham’s broader cabinet changes, which also include John Healey taking over from Rachel Reeves as chancellor of the exchequer. Healey previously served as defence secretary, and his appointment to the Treasury comes at a pivotal time for the UK economy.

Challenges Ahead for the Housing Market

The housing market faces significant challenges in the coming months, despite starting the year with cautious optimism. Global economic uncertainties have created hurdles, particularly in terms of inflation and interest rates. While inflation has shown signs of moving closer to its target, and base rates have remained steady, there is still much work to be done to stabilise the economy.

Nathan Emerson, CEO of Propertymark, highlighted the importance of housing to economic growth. He noted that key areas such as stamp duty for property buyers in England and Northern Ireland, as well as taxation frameworks affecting private landlords, require careful review and potential reform. These issues could play a crucial role in shaping the future of the housing market.

What This Means for Homeowners and Buyers

Rayner’s reappointment could signal a renewed focus on housing policy under Burnham’s leadership. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to keep an eye on potential changes to stamp duty or other property-related taxes, which could impact affordability and market activity.

For landlords in the private rented sector, the government’s approach to taxation frameworks will be particularly significant. Any reforms could influence the viability of buy-to-let investments and the availability of rental properties.

As the new chancellor, Healey will also play a key role in shaping economic policy, with housing likely to remain a central pillar of his strategy. Borrowers might consider reviewing their mortgage options in light of any potential shifts in interest rates or economic conditions.

The coming months will be critical for the housing market, with both Rayner and Healey poised to tackle ongoing challenges. With inflation and global uncertainty still factors, homeowners and buyers are encouraged to stay informed about policy developments that could affect their financial decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Angela Rayner has been reappointed as housing secretary in Prime Minister Andy Burnham’s new cabinet. This marks her return to the role after stepping down in September 2025 due to a stamp duty issue linked to her property purchase in Hove. Following an investigation by HMRC earlier this year, Rayner was cleared of any deliberate wrongdoing or negligence and has since repaid approximately £40,000 in underpaid stamp duty. She now replaces Steve Reed as secretary of state for housing, communities and local government.

This reshuffle comes as part of Burnham’s broader cabinet changes, which also include John Healey taking over from Rachel Reeves as chancellor of the exchequer. Healey previously served as defence secretary, and his appointment to the Treasury comes at a pivotal time for the UK economy.

Challenges Ahead for the Housing Market

The housing market faces significant challenges in the coming months, despite starting the year with cautious optimism. Global economic uncertainties have created hurdles, particularly in terms of inflation and interest rates. While inflation has shown signs of moving closer to its target, and base rates have remained steady, there is still much work to be done to stabilise the economy.

Nathan Emerson, CEO of Propertymark, highlighted the importance of housing to economic growth. He noted that key areas such as stamp duty for property buyers in England and Northern Ireland, as well as taxation frameworks affecting private landlords, require careful review and potential reform. These issues could play a crucial role in shaping the future of the housing market.

What This Means for Homeowners and Buyers

Rayner’s reappointment could signal a renewed focus on housing policy under Burnham’s leadership. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to keep an eye on potential changes to stamp duty or other property-related taxes, which could impact affordability and market activity.

For landlords in the private rented sector, the government’s approach to taxation frameworks will be particularly significant. Any reforms could influence the viability of buy-to-let investments and the availability of rental properties.

As the new chancellor, Healey will also play a key role in shaping economic policy, with housing likely to remain a central pillar of his strategy. Borrowers might consider reviewing their mortgage options in light of any potential shifts in interest rates or economic conditions.

The coming months will be critical for the housing market, with both Rayner and Healey poised to tackle ongoing challenges. With inflation and global uncertainty still factors, homeowners and buyers are encouraged to stay informed about policy developments that could affect their financial decisions.

item.title: Property industry reacts to new prime minister
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The property industry has begun responding to Andy Burnham’s appointment as the UK’s new prime minister, with sector leaders highlighting housing priorities and potential policy directions under the incoming Labour government. Burnham, who succeeded Keir Starmer this week, faces significant challenges around housing supply, affordability and market fluidity.

Housing supply and affordability in focus

Industry bodies have emphasised the need for clear strategies to address England’s chronic housing shortage. Propertymark’s CEO Nathan Emerson welcomed Burnham’s leadership while stressing the importance of delivering 1.5 million homes – a key Labour manifesto pledge. Emerson noted that achieving this target would require regionally tailored approaches alongside infrastructure investment, moving beyond simple housebuilding targets to create “well-designed, functional communities”.

Planning experts have highlighted the potential role of council housing in addressing affordability challenges. Tony Aitken of Colliers pointed to Burnham’s ambition for record affordable housing delivery, suggesting this would need both policy clarity through National Planning Policy Framework updates and substantial public funding. Aitken referenced Manchester’s Good Growth Fund as a potential model for ensuring new developments include necessary infrastructure from the outset.

Market fluidity and economic concerns

Beyond new construction, industry figures have raised concerns about transaction volumes in the existing housing market. Jackson-Stops chairman Nick Leeming noted that while increasing housing supply remains crucial, supporting market movement is equally important. Recent research suggests stamp duty reforms could incentivise hundreds of thousands of homeowners to sell, potentially improving market liquidity.

Economic uncertainty continues to impact housing decisions, with 42% of delayed movers citing this as a factor in postponing transactions. Industry observers will be watching for early signals of how the new government plans to balance housing delivery with measures to support market confidence and transaction activity.

As the property sector engages with the new administration, key themes emerging include the need for regional housing strategies, infrastructure investment and policies that address both supply and demand-side challenges. The coming months may reveal whether Burnham’s government can translate housing ambitions into deliverable plans.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The property industry has begun responding to Andy Burnham’s appointment as the UK’s new prime minister, with sector leaders highlighting housing priorities and potential policy directions under the incoming Labour government. Burnham, who succeeded Keir Starmer this week, faces significant challenges around housing supply, affordability and market fluidity.

Housing supply and affordability in focus

Industry bodies have emphasised the need for clear strategies to address England’s chronic housing shortage. Propertymark’s CEO Nathan Emerson welcomed Burnham’s leadership while stressing the importance of delivering 1.5 million homes – a key Labour manifesto pledge. Emerson noted that achieving this target would require regionally tailored approaches alongside infrastructure investment, moving beyond simple housebuilding targets to create “well-designed, functional communities”.

Planning experts have highlighted the potential role of council housing in addressing affordability challenges. Tony Aitken of Colliers pointed to Burnham’s ambition for record affordable housing delivery, suggesting this would need both policy clarity through National Planning Policy Framework updates and substantial public funding. Aitken referenced Manchester’s Good Growth Fund as a potential model for ensuring new developments include necessary infrastructure from the outset.

Market fluidity and economic concerns

Beyond new construction, industry figures have raised concerns about transaction volumes in the existing housing market. Jackson-Stops chairman Nick Leeming noted that while increasing housing supply remains crucial, supporting market movement is equally important. Recent research suggests stamp duty reforms could incentivise hundreds of thousands of homeowners to sell, potentially improving market liquidity.

Economic uncertainty continues to impact housing decisions, with 42% of delayed movers citing this as a factor in postponing transactions. Industry observers will be watching for early signals of how the new government plans to balance housing delivery with measures to support market confidence and transaction activity.

As the property sector engages with the new administration, key themes emerging include the need for regional housing strategies, infrastructure investment and policies that address both supply and demand-side challenges. The coming months may reveal whether Burnham’s government can translate housing ambitions into deliverable plans.

item.title: Panorama highlights threat of criminal gangs targeting rental properties
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A recent BBC Panorama investigation has exposed how organised criminal groups are exploiting the rental market, using sophisticated fraud tactics to take control of properties. The documentary, titled There’s a Gangster in My Flat, revealed shocking cases where landlords discovered their homes had been transformed into illegal drug operations, brothels, or unlawfully sublet properties after falling victim to tenancy fraud.

The growing threat of rental fraud

The programme highlighted how criminals are increasingly using fake identities, forged references, and fraudulent documents to secure tenancies. Paul Shamplina of Landlord Action, who contributed to the investigation, explained that these cases go beyond typical tenant disputes. "Professional rogue tenants often move between properties systematically, leaving behind substantial rent arrears and property damage while evading consequences," he noted.

Landlords featured in the documentary shared harrowing experiences of discovering their properties being used for criminal activities, only to face months of legal battles to regain possession. In some instances, properties suffered severe structural damage, requiring costly renovations before they could be re-let.

Challenges in tackling rental fraud

The investigation also examined why reports of criminal activity in rental properties frequently fail to result in prosecutions. Shamplina pointed out the gap between criminal and civil processes: "When fraud is suspected, landlords often find themselves trapped between unresponsive police investigations and lengthy possession proceedings through the courts."

Key challenges identified include:

  • Fraud reports sometimes being treated as civil disputes rather than criminal matters
  • Criminals using multiple false identities to bypass referencing checks
  • The slow pace of possession proceedings allowing damage to escalate
  • Difficulty recovering financial losses even after regaining property access

Shamplina emphasised that while thorough tenant referencing remains essential, it cannot guarantee protection against determined fraudsters. "Vigilance throughout the tenancy is crucial," he advised. "Landlords and agents should monitor for warning signs like unexpected visitors, property alterations, or requests to pay rent in cash."

The documentary serves as a stark reminder of the risks facing private landlords. With rental fraud becoming increasingly sophisticated, property owners may wish to review their vetting processes and consider specialist landlord insurance that covers malicious damage and legal expenses. Early action at the first sign of suspicious activity could help prevent situations from escalating into costly, traumatic ordeals.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A recent BBC Panorama investigation has exposed how organised criminal groups are exploiting the rental market, using sophisticated fraud tactics to take control of properties. The documentary, titled There’s a Gangster in My Flat, revealed shocking cases where landlords discovered their homes had been transformed into illegal drug operations, brothels, or unlawfully sublet properties after falling victim to tenancy fraud.

The growing threat of rental fraud

The programme highlighted how criminals are increasingly using fake identities, forged references, and fraudulent documents to secure tenancies. Paul Shamplina of Landlord Action, who contributed to the investigation, explained that these cases go beyond typical tenant disputes. "Professional rogue tenants often move between properties systematically, leaving behind substantial rent arrears and property damage while evading consequences," he noted.

Landlords featured in the documentary shared harrowing experiences of discovering their properties being used for criminal activities, only to face months of legal battles to regain possession. In some instances, properties suffered severe structural damage, requiring costly renovations before they could be re-let.

Challenges in tackling rental fraud

The investigation also examined why reports of criminal activity in rental properties frequently fail to result in prosecutions. Shamplina pointed out the gap between criminal and civil processes: "When fraud is suspected, landlords often find themselves trapped between unresponsive police investigations and lengthy possession proceedings through the courts."

Key challenges identified include:

  • Fraud reports sometimes being treated as civil disputes rather than criminal matters
  • Criminals using multiple false identities to bypass referencing checks
  • The slow pace of possession proceedings allowing damage to escalate
  • Difficulty recovering financial losses even after regaining property access

Shamplina emphasised that while thorough tenant referencing remains essential, it cannot guarantee protection against determined fraudsters. "Vigilance throughout the tenancy is crucial," he advised. "Landlords and agents should monitor for warning signs like unexpected visitors, property alterations, or requests to pay rent in cash."

The documentary serves as a stark reminder of the risks facing private landlords. With rental fraud becoming increasingly sophisticated, property owners may wish to review their vetting processes and consider specialist landlord insurance that covers malicious damage and legal expenses. Early action at the first sign of suspicious activity could help prevent situations from escalating into costly, traumatic ordeals.

item.title: Prime rents rise as landlords respond to higher costs and Renters’ Rights Act
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Prime rental markets across the UK have seen consistent price growth in mid-2026, with landlords adjusting rents upwards to counterbalance rising costs and regulatory changes. New data from Savills reveals quarterly increases of 1.3% in prime regional markets and 1.2% in outer prime London, while prime central London experienced a more modest 0.4% uplift.

Regional variations in rental growth

The South West emerged as the strongest performer, with rents climbing 1.6%, closely followed by West London at 1.4%. Specific neighbourhoods including Fulham, Chiswick and Wandsworth experienced particularly pronounced rental growth. The Cotswolds and regional towns also showed robust performance, with quarterly increases between 1.7% and 1.9%.

Properties affected by the Renters' Rights Act (RRA) have seen sharper rent rises compared to higher-value homes. In prime central London, sub-£100,000 properties recorded 0.7% growth versus just 0.1% for premium rentals. A similar pattern emerged in outer prime London, where RRA-affected properties saw annual growth of 2.7% against 1.7% for those above the threshold.

Landlords adapt to changing market conditions

Industry analysts suggest multiple factors are driving rental increases. Jessica Tomlinson, research analyst at Savills, notes: "Landlords face a perfect storm of higher mortgage costs, increased taxation and regulatory changes under the RRA. Many are reassessing portfolio valuations to maintain profitability amidst these pressures."

The RRA's abolition of Section 21 notices appears particularly significant, cited by 48% of London agents and 71% elsewhere as landlords' primary concern. This regulatory shift has reportedly prompted some landlords to consider selling properties, further tightening rental supply.

Market expectations reveal a stark divide between landlords and tenants. While 82% of London agents report landlords anticipating rent rises, only 30% of tenants share this expectation. This disconnect suggests potential challenges ahead for rental negotiations and affordability.

As the market continues to evolve, both landlords and tenants may need to stay informed about these shifting dynamics. The interplay between regulatory changes, economic pressures and local market conditions appears set to shape the rental landscape throughout 2026.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Prime rental markets across the UK have seen consistent price growth in mid-2026, with landlords adjusting rents upwards to counterbalance rising costs and regulatory changes. New data from Savills reveals quarterly increases of 1.3% in prime regional markets and 1.2% in outer prime London, while prime central London experienced a more modest 0.4% uplift.

Regional variations in rental growth

The South West emerged as the strongest performer, with rents climbing 1.6%, closely followed by West London at 1.4%. Specific neighbourhoods including Fulham, Chiswick and Wandsworth experienced particularly pronounced rental growth. The Cotswolds and regional towns also showed robust performance, with quarterly increases between 1.7% and 1.9%.

Properties affected by the Renters' Rights Act (RRA) have seen sharper rent rises compared to higher-value homes. In prime central London, sub-£100,000 properties recorded 0.7% growth versus just 0.1% for premium rentals. A similar pattern emerged in outer prime London, where RRA-affected properties saw annual growth of 2.7% against 1.7% for those above the threshold.

Landlords adapt to changing market conditions

Industry analysts suggest multiple factors are driving rental increases. Jessica Tomlinson, research analyst at Savills, notes: "Landlords face a perfect storm of higher mortgage costs, increased taxation and regulatory changes under the RRA. Many are reassessing portfolio valuations to maintain profitability amidst these pressures."

The RRA's abolition of Section 21 notices appears particularly significant, cited by 48% of London agents and 71% elsewhere as landlords' primary concern. This regulatory shift has reportedly prompted some landlords to consider selling properties, further tightening rental supply.

Market expectations reveal a stark divide between landlords and tenants. While 82% of London agents report landlords anticipating rent rises, only 30% of tenants share this expectation. This disconnect suggests potential challenges ahead for rental negotiations and affordability.

As the market continues to evolve, both landlords and tenants may need to stay informed about these shifting dynamics. The interplay between regulatory changes, economic pressures and local market conditions appears set to shape the rental landscape throughout 2026.

item.title: Software integration offers property managers live repair updates
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property managers and letting agents could benefit from streamlined repair tracking following a new integration between maintenance platform Fixflo and contractor management system BigChange. The partnership aims to reduce administrative burdens by automating updates across both platforms, providing real-time visibility of repair progress from initial instruction through to final invoicing.

How the integration works

The connection between the two systems automatically shares key information without requiring manual input. When contractors using BigChange schedule or reschedule appointments, these changes immediately reflect in Fixflo's platform. Property professionals can monitor job progress without needing to chase contractors for updates, while tenants receive automatic notifications about upcoming visits.

The integration also handles financial aspects of repair work, with completed job dates, invoices and costs transferring seamlessly between systems. The entire workflow – including quotes, approvals and requests for additional work – becomes visible within a single interface. According to the companies, this creates a complete audit trail for each repair job while reducing opportunities for errors or miscommunication.

Potential benefits for property professionals

Dean Falvey, head of contractor operations at Fixflo, explained that much of the frustration in property repairs stems from coordination rather than the actual work: "The contractor works in their own system, and the agent sees it all update in Fixflo." This real-time synchronisation could help property managers by:

  • Eliminating the need to manually request updates from contractors
  • Providing tenants with accurate appointment information
  • Automating invoice reconciliation between systems
  • Creating a transparent record of all repair activities

The integration is currently available to customers using both platforms, potentially offering a solution to one of the most time-consuming aspects of property management. While the technology won't speed up actual repair work, it may significantly reduce the administrative overhead surrounding maintenance issues.

For property professionals juggling multiple repair jobs across different properties, such automated tracking systems could prove invaluable in maintaining tenant satisfaction while keeping operational costs under control. As property management becomes increasingly digitised, integrations like this may become standard practice in the industry.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property managers and letting agents could benefit from streamlined repair tracking following a new integration between maintenance platform Fixflo and contractor management system BigChange. The partnership aims to reduce administrative burdens by automating updates across both platforms, providing real-time visibility of repair progress from initial instruction through to final invoicing.

The connection between the two systems automatically shares key information without requiring manual input. When contractors using BigChange schedule or reschedule appointments, these changes immediately reflect in Fixflo's platform. Property professionals can monitor job progress without needing to chase contractors for updates, while tenants receive automatic notifications about upcoming visits.

The integration also handles financial aspects of repair work, with completed job dates, invoices and costs transferring seamlessly between systems. The entire workflow – including quotes, approvals and requests for additional work – becomes visible within a single interface. According to the companies, this creates a complete audit trail for each repair job while reducing opportunities for errors or miscommunication.

Dean Falvey, head of contractor operations at Fixflo, explained that much of the frustration in property repairs stems from coordination rather than the actual work: "The contractor works in their own system, and the agent sees it all update in Fixflo." This real-time synchronisation could help property managers by:

  • Providing tenants with accurate appointment information
  • Automating invoice reconciliation between systems
  • Creating a transparent record of all repair activities

The integration is currently available to customers using both platforms, potentially offering a solution to one of the most time-consuming aspects of property management. While the technology won't speed up actual repair work, it may significantly reduce the administrative overhead surrounding maintenance issues.

For property professionals juggling multiple repair jobs across different properties, such automated tracking systems could prove invaluable in maintaining tenant satisfaction while keeping operational costs under control. As property management becomes increasingly digitised, integrations like this may become standard practice in the industry.

item.title: Asking prices drop 1% in July
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market has seen a slight dip in asking prices this July, with a 1% decrease reported by Rightmove. The property portal attributes this decline to a combination of factors, including soaring summer temperatures and the distraction of major sporting events like the World Cup. Whilst this may raise questions for potential sellers, it could present opportunities for buyers looking to enter the market.

What’s Behind the Drop?

Rightmove’s latest data suggests that the summer heatwave and the excitement surrounding the World Cup have contributed to a temporary slowdown in buyer activity. Historically, the summer months often see a lull in property transactions as people prioritise holidays and outdoor activities over house hunting. This year, the added distraction of global football has further dampened demand, leading to a modest reduction in asking prices.

Experts note that such seasonal fluctuations are not uncommon and often balance out over the course of the year. However, homeowners considering selling might want to take these trends into account when planning their next steps. Pricing strategies could play a crucial role in attracting interest during quieter periods.

Opportunities for Buyers

For prospective buyers, the dip in asking prices could be a chance to secure a property at a more favourable rate. With fewer buyers actively searching during this period, competition may be reduced, potentially giving those in the market an edge in negotiations. It could also be worth exploring properties that have been on the market for a while, as sellers may be more open to offers.

That said, it’s important to approach any purchase with caution. Whilst lower asking prices might seem appealing, buyers should ensure they’re making informed decisions based on thorough research and affordability checks. Consulting with a mortgage advisor could help clarify options and ensure financial readiness.

As the summer draws to a close, the housing market is likely to regain momentum. Whether you’re buying or selling, staying informed about market trends and seeking professional guidance could help navigate the ever-changing property landscape with confidence.

By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market has seen a slight dip in asking prices this July, with a 1% decrease reported by Rightmove. The property portal attributes this decline to a combination of factors, including soaring summer temperatures and the distraction of major sporting events like the World Cup. Rightmove’s latest data suggests that the summer heatwave and the excitement surrounding the World Cup have contributed to a temporary slowdown in buyer activity. Historically, the summer months often see a lull in property transactions as people prioritise holidays and outdoor activities over house hunting. This year, the added distraction of global football has further dampened demand, leading to a modest reduction in asking prices.

Experts note that such seasonal fluctuations are not uncommon and often balance out over the course of the year. That said, it’s important to approach any purchase with caution. Whilst lower asking prices might seem appealing, buyers should ensure they’re making informed decisions based on thorough research and affordability checks. Consulting with a mortgage advisor could help clarify options and ensure financial readiness.

As the summer draws to a close, the housing market is likely to regain momentum. Whether you’re buying or selling, staying informed about market trends and seeking professional guidance could help navigate the ever-changing property landscape with confidence.

item.title: BTL figures show ‘incredible resilience’
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The buy-to-let (BTL) market continues to demonstrate remarkable strength, with recent figures highlighting increased lending activity, robust yields, and low levels of arrears. These trends suggest sustained confidence among property investors, despite ongoing economic challenges and regulatory changes in the sector.

Growing Lending Activity

Recent data indicates a notable rise in BTL lending, reflecting renewed interest from both new and experienced landlords. This uptick in borrowing could be attributed to competitive mortgage rates and the potential for long-term returns in the property market. Lenders have also been expanding their product offerings, providing landlords with a wider range of options to suit their investment strategies.

Strong Rental Yields

Rental yields remain a key driver of investor confidence, with many regions across the UK reporting steady or even increased returns. Whilst property prices in some areas have stabilised or dipped slightly, demand for rental properties continues to grow, particularly in urban centres and areas with strong employment markets. This sustained demand allows landlords to maintain healthy cash flow, making BTL investments an attractive proposition for many.

Low Arrears and Market Stability

Another positive indicator for the BTL sector is the low level of arrears reported across mortgage portfolios. This suggests that landlords are managing their finances effectively, even in a challenging economic climate. Additionally, the stability of the rental market has provided landlords with a reliable income stream, further reinforcing the resilience of the sector.

Whilst the BTL market faces ongoing regulatory changes and economic uncertainties, the latest figures paint a picture of sustained investor confidence. Borrowers considering entering the market or expanding their portfolios might wish to explore the current range of mortgage products available, whilst keeping a close eye on regional yield trends and market conditions.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The buy-to-let (BTL) market continues to demonstrate remarkable strength, with recent figures highlighting increased lending activity, robust yields, and low levels of arrears. These trends suggest sustained confidence among property investors, despite ongoing economic challenges and regulatory changes in the sector.

Growing Lending Activity

Recent data indicates a notable rise in BTL lending, reflecting renewed interest from both new and experienced landlords. This uptick in borrowing could be attributed to competitive mortgage rates and the potential for long-term returns in the property market. Lenders have also been expanding their product offerings, providing landlords with a wider range of options to suit their investment strategies.

Rental yields remain a key driver of investor confidence, with many regions across the UK reporting steady or even increased returns. This sustained demand allows landlords to maintain healthy cash flow, making BTL investments an attractive proposition for many.

This suggests that landlords are managing their finances effectively, even in a challenging economic climate. Additionally, the stability of the rental market has provided landlords with a reliable income stream, further reinforcing the resilience of the sector.

Whilst the BTL market faces ongoing regulatory changes and economic uncertainties, the latest figures paint a picture of sustained investor confidence. Borrowers considering entering the market or expanding their portfolios might wish to explore the current range of mortgage products available, whilst keeping a close eye on regional yield trends and market conditions.

item.title: Investors are becoming more selective
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK property investors are adopting a more discerning approach to their portfolios, with recent trends showing a clear shift towards assets offering stronger income potential and resilience against economic headwinds. This strategic pivot comes as market conditions prompt investors to prioritise sectors benefiting from fundamental demographic demand and reliable cash flow.

The rise of income-focused investment strategies

Market analysts note a growing preference for property types that deliver consistent rental yields, particularly in sectors less vulnerable to economic downturns. Purpose-built student accommodation, healthcare properties and strategically located logistics hubs are attracting particular attention from investors seeking stable returns.

Several factors appear to be driving this selective approach:

  • Ongoing economic uncertainty prompting more cautious investment decisions
  • Demographic shifts creating sustained demand in specific property sectors
  • The search for inflation-resistant assets amid rising costs
  • Changing tenant preferences following pandemic-driven behavioural shifts

Regional variations in investment appeal

Geographical selectivity has also become more pronounced, with investors increasingly focusing on locations demonstrating strong employment growth and infrastructure development. Northern cities with lower entry costs but solid rental demand are competing with London's prime markets for investor attention.

Property professionals suggest this trend reflects a broader move towards fundamentals-based investing, where thorough due diligence and local market knowledge are becoming more valuable than speculative approaches. Investors might consider reviewing their portfolios to assess exposure to sectors with strong underlying demand drivers.

As the market continues to evolve, this selective approach may help investors navigate potential challenges while positioning for long-term growth. The current climate underscores the importance of careful research and professional advice when making property investment decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

UK property investors are adopting a more discerning approach to their portfolios, with recent trends showing a clear shift towards assets offering stronger income potential and resilience against economic headwinds. This strategic pivot comes as market conditions prompt investors to prioritise sectors benefiting from fundamental demographic demand and reliable cash flow.

The rise of income-focused investment strategies

Market analysts note a growing preference for property types that deliver consistent rental yields, particularly in sectors less vulnerable to economic downturns. Purpose-built student accommodation, healthcare properties and strategically located logistics hubs are attracting particular attention from investors seeking stable returns.

Several factors appear to be driving this selective approach:

  • Ongoing economic uncertainty prompting more cautious investment decisions
  • Demographic shifts creating sustained demand in specific property sectors
  • The search for inflation-resistant assets amid rising costs
  • Changing tenant preferences following pandemic-driven behavioural shifts

Regional variations in investment appeal

Geographical selectivity has also become more pronounced, with investors increasingly focusing on locations demonstrating strong employment growth and infrastructure development. Northern cities with lower entry costs but solid rental demand are competing with London's prime markets for investor attention.

Property professionals suggest this trend reflects a broader move towards fundamentals-based investing, where thorough due diligence and local market knowledge are becoming more valuable than speculative approaches. Investors might consider reviewing their portfolios to assess exposure to sectors with strong underlying demand drivers.

As the market continues to evolve, this selective approach may help investors navigate potential challenges while positioning for long-term growth. The current climate underscores the importance of careful research and professional advice when making property investment decisions.

item.title: Escalating service charges are biggest concern
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Rising service charges have emerged as a significant concern for homeowners and property investors across the UK, according to recent survey findings. With costs for property maintenance and management escalating, many are calling for greater transparency and more control over these expenses. This issue is particularly pressing for those living in leasehold properties, where service charges can form a substantial part of annual housing costs.

The impact of rising service charges

Service charges cover essential costs such as building maintenance, cleaning, insurance, and repairs in shared properties. However, many homeowners have reported sharp increases in these fees, often without clear justification. This trend can place additional financial strain on residents, particularly those on fixed incomes or with tight budgets. For property investors, escalating service charges can also affect rental yields and overall returns.

Some leaseholders have expressed frustration at the lack of control they have over these costs, as decisions about maintenance and service providers are typically made by freeholders or managing agents. Without proper oversight, there is a risk that charges could become disproportionate to the services provided.

Calling for greater transparency

The survey findings have prompted calls for improved transparency and accountability in how service charges are calculated and managed. Homeowners and industry experts alike are advocating for clearer communication from managing agents and freeholders, ensuring that residents understand what they are paying for and why costs may be rising.

  • Detailed breakdowns of service charge costs.
  • Regular updates on planned maintenance works.
  • Opportunities for leaseholders to scrutinise and challenge charges.

Some suggest that regulatory changes may be needed to empower leaseholders and ensure fairness in the way service charges are administered. This could include stricter guidelines for managing agents and greater rights for homeowners to influence decisions about property management.

As service charges continue to climb, homeowners and investors may wish to thoroughly review their costs and seek advice if they believe charges are excessive. Greater transparency and control over these expenses could help alleviate some of the financial pressures faced by residents and ensure that property management remains fair and affordable.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Rising service charges have emerged as a significant concern for homeowners and property investors across the UK, according to recent survey findings. With costs for property maintenance and management escalating, many are calling for greater transparency and more control over these expenses. This issue is particularly pressing for those living in leasehold properties, where service charges can form a substantial part of annual housing costs.
